category_summary:
The AI community was dominated by the **OpenAI GPT-5.5** launch, described as ['a new class of intelligence'](/?date=2026-04-24&category=social#item-785f5b0f6024) for agentic work. **Sam Altman** [confirmed rollout for Plus, Pro](/?date=2026-04-24&category=social#item-a0f4c60333e0), Business, and Enterprise users, with [API pricing at $5/$30](/?date=2026-04-24&category=social#item-3aa8656b1b4a) per million tokens and a 1M context window. **Ethan Mollick** [called it the best model](/?date=2026-04-24&category=social#item-853a0da5ea0a) for hard problems after weeks of testing, noting it autonomously conducted social science research. **Swyx** provided the most [comprehensive technical breakdown](/?date=2026-04-24&category=social#item-2b671c1e63d6), highlighting 400K Codex context, co-design with **NVIDIA** GB200 hardware, and new Pareto frontiers across benchmarks.

- **Allie K Miller** [shared detailed benchmarks](/?date=2026-04-24&category=social#item-d6bf48b0239d): 82.7% Terminal-Bench 2.0, 84.9% GDPval, 85% ARC. **Simon Willison** [noted GPT-5.5 is not yet](/?date=2026-04-24&category=social#item-92eb6da047ce) in the standard OpenAI API, adding context about the **Anthropic** OpenClaw rivalry.
- **NVIDIA** [announced a partnership claiming](/?date=2026-04-24&category=social#item-e811a1914ddd) 35x token cost reduction via GB200 NVL72 systems, with **Altman** [revealing NVIDIA as the first](/?date=2026-04-24&category=social#item-4fd9cd8b155a) whole-company **Codex** deployment.
- Beyond GPT-5.5, **xAI** [launched **Grok Voice Think Fast 1.0**](/?date=2026-04-24&category=social#item-e447385c79f1), claiming #1 on Tau Voice Bench for complex voice workflows.
- **Anthropic** [published a post-mortem](/?date=2026-04-24&category=social#item-e65402acd1ff) on **Claude Code** quality degradation dating to March 4, confirmed by **Boris Cherny**. Community reactions were pointed, with **levelsio** noting they had [flagged the issue early](/?date=2026-04-24&category=social#item-6a0f6815a8a5).
- **Nathan Lambert** [offered a standout policy analysis](/?date=2026-04-24&category=social#item-abf5a83df6e1) arguing proposed bans on Chinese AI distillation would hurt US open-source ecosystems and consolidate power among closed labs.
category_summary_html:
The AI community was dominated by the OpenAI GPT-5.5 launch, described as 'a new class of intelligence' for agentic work. Sam Altman confirmed rollout for Plus, Pro, Business, and Enterprise users, with API pricing at $5/$30 per million tokens and a 1M context window. Ethan Mollick called it the best model for hard problems after weeks of testing, noting it autonomously conducted social science research. Swyx provided the most comprehensive technical breakdown, highlighting 400K Codex context, co-design with NVIDIA GB200 hardware, and new Pareto frontiers across benchmarks.

  • Allie K Miller shared detailed benchmarks: 82.7% Terminal-Bench 2.0, 84.9% GDPval, 85% ARC. Simon Willison noted GPT-5.5 is not yet in the standard OpenAI API, adding context about the Anthropic OpenClaw rivalry.
  • NVIDIA announced a partnership claiming 35x token cost reduction via GB200 NVL72 systems, with Altman revealing NVIDIA as the first whole-company Codex deployment.
  • Beyond GPT-5.5, xAI launched Grok Voice Think Fast 1.0, claiming #1 on Tau Voice Bench for complex voice workflows.
  • Anthropic published a post-mortem on Claude Code quality degradation dating to March 4, confirmed by Boris Cherny. Community reactions were pointed, with levelsio noting they had flagged the issue early.
  • Nathan Lambert offered a standout policy analysis arguing proposed bans on Chinese AI distillation would hurt US open-source ecosystems and consolidate power among closed labs.

themes.name: GPT-5.5 Launch
themes.description: OpenAI releases GPT-5.5, described as 'a new class of intelligence' optimized for agentic work, matching 5.4 speed with fewer tokens per task. API at $5/$30 per million input/output tokens with 1M context. Available in ChatGPT and Codex, with Pro tier. Sam Altman emphasizes iterative deployment and democratization.

themes.name: GPT-5.5 Release and Evaluation
themes.description: Breaking coverage of GPT-5.5 release. Mollick reports it as the best model for hard problems after weeks of testing. Willison notes it's available but not yet in the standard API, accessible via Codex backend. Both provide hands-on assessments.

themes.name: AI Distillation Policy Debate
themes.description: Nathan Lambert provides extensive analysis on proposed bans of Chinese AI distillation, arguing it would harm US open-source ecosystem, startups like Cursor, and academic research while consolidating power to closed labs.

themes.name: Claude Code Quality Post-Mortem
themes.description: Anthropic (Boris Cherny) published a post-mortem on Claude Code quality degradation. Issues confirmed dating back to March 4. Opus 4.7 also reported as problematic. Usage limits being reset for subscribers. The most complex investigation the team has conducted.

themes.name: Grok Voice Think Fast 1.0 Launch
themes.description: xAI launches Grok Voice Think Fast 1.0, claiming #1 on Tau Voice Bench with superior handling of noise, accents, and interruptions for multi-step voice workflows.

themes.name: NVIDIA-OpenAI Partnership
themes.description: Deep partnership between NVIDIA and OpenAI for GPT-5.5, including co-design with GB200/GB300 NVL72 systems, 35x token cost reduction, and NVIDIA internal adoption of Codex agents.

themes.name: AI Model Competition and Ecosystem
themes.description: Discussion of the competitive landscape between OpenAI and Anthropic, including Mollick's call for calm amid release frenzy, validation of Claude Opus 4.7, and references to the 'OpenClaw' competition between the companies.

themes.name: Google TPU v8 and Distributed Training
themes.description: Google announces TPU 8t (training, 9600 chips, 121 exaflops/pod) and TPU 8i (inference, 1152 chips, optimized for low latency). Separately, Decoupled DiLoCo enables fault-tolerant geo-distributed training across heterogeneous hardware.

themes.name: GPT-5.5 Early Reviews
themes.description: Matt Shumer shares extensive review of GPT-5.5 after weeks of use. Calls it a massive leap but says 99% of users won't notice. Claims it outperforms Opus except for frontend. More token-efficient. Has one significant regression. Signals imminent GPT-5.5 release.

themes.name: Codex Enterprise Rollout
themes.description: OpenAI expands Codex to whole companies, piloting with NVIDIA. GPT-5.5 extends Codex beyond coding to general computer work including spreadsheets and slides.

items.title:
🚨 OpenAI just launched GPT-5.5.

The OpenAI team was nice enough to give me early access over the la...
items.content:
🚨 OpenAI just launched GPT-5.5.

The OpenAI team was nice enough to give me early access over the last several weeks, and I just want to flag: there is a certain class of models (one that we’re hitting now) where unless you are deep in code or scientific research, you might not even notice a difference.

The vast majority of business professionals may only notice that these models are concise as hell.

They’re direct in a way that may actually make you sit back.

They’re token efficient.

And while they might not be the greatest copywriters of all time, boy can they impact system connections.

@OpenAI is calling the new model "a new class of intelligence for real work," with the biggest gains in coding, computer use, knowledge work, and research.

Agentic coding → 82.7% on Terminal-Bench 2.0 (up from 75.1% on 5.4, and ahead of Claude Opus 4.7 at 69.4%). Translation: it can plan, use tools, hold context, and finish multi-step technical work without hand-holding.

Knowledge work → 84.9% on GDPval, ahead of Claude Opus 4.7 and Gemini 3.1 Pro. 78.7% on OSWorld-Verified for operating real computer environments. OpenAI says 85% of their own company now uses Codex every week across finance, comms, marketing, and product. Their finance team reviewed 24,771 K-1 tax forms (71,637 pages) two weeks faster than last year - sheesh. A GTM employee automated weekly reporting and immediately got 5-10 hours a week back.

Scientific research → 85% on ARC-AGI-2 (up from 73.3%). 35.4% on FrontierMath Tier 4. A Jackson Lab researcher used it to analyze 62 samples and nearly 28,000 genes in work that would have taken his team months.

GPT-5.5 also matches GPT-5.4 per-token latency while performing at a much higher level of intelligence, and uses significantly fewer tokens to finish the same Codex tasks.

AVAILABILITY in ChatGPT: Plus, Pro, Business, Enterprise as of today. GPT-5.5 Pro for Pro, Business, Enterprise.

Docs and rollout info: https://t.co/5r7iyrPF4D

I am expecting more data silos to break in the enterprise and more scientific breakthroughs in the next 2-3 years.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Building on yesterday's [Reddit](/?date=2026-04-23&category=reddit#item-4310761fc0b2) buzz about an imminent OpenAI launch, Allie K Miller provides a detailed breakdown of OpenAI's GPT-5.5 launch, including benchmark results (82.7% Terminal-Bench 2.0, 84.9% GDPval, 85% ARC-AGI-2), enterprise use cases, availability tiers, and her assessment that we're entering a model class where improvements are less noticeable for non-technical users.
items.reasoning: This is the most substantive post in the batch - a breaking news announcement of GPT-5.5 with concrete benchmarks, availability details, and real-world enterprise deployment data. Allie Miller is a recognized AI industry voice with early access. This is a major new model release not yet in the ecosystem grounding.

items.title: Efficiency isn't just about speed anymore — it's about the massive reduction in the cost of intellig...
items.content:
Efficiency isn't just about speed anymore — it's about the massive reduction in the cost of intelligence. 

NVIDIA and @OpenAI's partnership leverages the GB200 NVL72 to deliver a 35x reduction in token costs, bringing enterprise-grade AI to an unprecedented scale.

Trained and served on NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 systems, GPT-5.5 delivers the sustained performance required for execution-heavy, multi-step work — and at NVIDIA, that means teams are now scaling human ingenuity with OpenAI Codex Agents.
items.author: @nvidia
items.summary: Building on yesterday's [Reddit](/?date=2026-04-23&category=reddit#item-4310761fc0b2) buzz about an imminent OpenAI launch, NVIDIA announces partnership with OpenAI on GB200 NVL72 systems for GPT-5.5, claiming 35x reduction in token costs. Notes GPT-5.5 is co-designed with GB200/GB300 and NVIDIA is using Codex Agents internally.
items.reasoning: Major partnership announcement with concrete technical details: 35x token cost reduction, GB200 NVL72 co-design, enterprise-grade scaling. Very high engagement (43K views, 974 likes). This is a significant industry development.

items.title: There's been a rapid increase in political chatter on the need to stop AI distillation "attacks". I'...
items.content:
There's been a rapid increase in political chatter on the need to stop AI distillation "attacks". I've been following this area closely, especially early legislation, as I see it being an area where initial action has pretty big unintended second order consequences.

At face value, I see the desire to ban Chinese models built on distillation (e.g. via entity listing or other interventions). I agree that the strength of leading AI companies, particularly OpenAI and Anthropic, is a massive strategic asset for the country.  The argument is that distillation limits their competitive position, where Chinese labs use distillation to "steal" capabilities and undercut them on price.

But, on balance so long as these distilled models are released openly with permissive licenses, the US AI ecosystem benefits massively by accessing them.  The U.S. has by far and away the biggest inference market, and having the option of cheaper, specialized open models to counterweight the best closed models is an excellent economic equilibrium driving investment and innovation at the frontier. We do not want to kneecap this dynamic in the middle of one of the most incredible times of rapid model progress.

To state the core of my worry clearly – we don't have clear evidence on the exact benefits Chinese companies gain from distillation. We have some evidence on HOW distillation data is accessed, only from the same companies likely championing this policy action. This doesn't map cleanly to impact. Some experts think distillation is becoming less relevant in the era of RL environments as training data, some others think distillation is becoming easier. We need to know the true effects before we consider siloing the US AI ecosystem out of the global, open ecosystem.

Flourishing startups like Cursor use these open-weight models as a central method for pushing their long-term independence in the ecosystem. Also, a large majority of academic research in the U.S. is built on Chinese models right now. Banning the Chinese open weight models right now will result in massive consolidation of power onto the closed AI labs right when the open ecosystem in the US is starting to explore and blossom more.

I worry it could be a sort of 6-12month delay in capability rollout of open models if such a ban was enacted, and long term make the open ecosystem not really viable. The open ecosystem is currently very fragile, supported by a few model builders. 

At the same time, lots of weird effects globally could follow this ban, where the US may not play a role in the global open model ecosystem. 

So if people want to discuss this more, happy to try and help. I think the interdependencies in the ecosystem aren't well communicated.
items.author: @natolambert
items.summary: Following [Social](/?date=2026-04-22&category=social#item-673deb810b60) discussion from two days ago on open-source AI restrictions, Nathan Lambert provides a comprehensive analysis of proposed bans on Chinese AI distillation. Argues that while protecting US frontier labs is important, banning Chinese open-weight models would harm US startups (like Cursor), academic research, and the fragile open-source ecosystem. continuation.reference_text: Following **Social** discussion from two days ago on open-source AI restrictions

items.summary_html: Following Social discussion from two days ago on open-source AI restrictions, Nathan Lambert provides a comprehensive analysis of proposed bans on Chinese AI distillation. Argues that while protecting US frontier labs is important, banning Chinese open-weight models would harm US startups (like Cursor), academic research, and the fragile open-source ecosystem. Warns of potential 6-12 month capability delay and consolidation of power to closed labs.
items.content_html:
There's been a rapid increase in political chatter on the need to stop AI distillation "attacks". I've been following this area closely, especially early legislation, as I see it being an area where initial action has pretty big unintended second order consequences.

At face value, I see the desire to ban Chinese models built on distillation (e.g. via entity listing or other interventions). I agree that the strength of leading AI companies, particularly OpenAI and Anthropic, is a massive strategic asset for the country. The argument is that distillation limits their competitive position, where Chinese labs use distillation to "steal" capabilities and undercut them on price.

But, on balance so long as these distilled models are released openly with permissive licenses, the US AI ecosystem benefits massively by accessing them. The U.S. has by far and away the biggest inference market, and having the option of cheaper, specialized open models to counterweight the best closed models is an excellent economic equilibrium driving investment and innovation at the frontier. We do not want to kneecap this dynamic in the middle of one of the most incredible times of rapid model progress.

To state the core of my worry clearly – we don't have clear evidence on the exact benefits Chinese companies gain from distillation. We have some evidence on HOW distillation data is accessed, only from the same companies likely championing this policy action. This doesn't map cleanly to impact. Some experts think distillation is becoming less relevant in the era of RL environments as training data, some others think distillation is becoming easier. We need to know the true effects before we consider siloing the US AI ecosystem out of the global, open ecosystem.

Flourishing startups like Cursor use these open-weight models as a central method for pushing their long-term independence in the ecosystem. Also, a large majority of academic research in the U.S. is built on Chinese models right now. Banning the Chinese open weight models right now will result in massive consolidation of power onto the closed AI labs right when the open ecosystem in the US is starting to explore and blossom more.

I worry it could be a sort of 6-12month delay in capability rollout of open models if such a ban was enacted, and long term make the open ecosystem not really viable. The open ecosystem is currently very fragile, supported by a few model builders.

At the same time, lots of weird effects globally could follow this ban, where the US may not play a role in the global open model ecosystem.

So if people want to discuss this more, happy to try and help. I think the interdependencies in the ecosystem aren't well communicated.

items.title: 1. We believe in iterative deployment; although GPT-5.5 is already a smart model, we expect rapid im...
items.content:
1. We believe in iterative deployment; although GPT-5.5 is already a smart model, we expect rapid improvements. Iterative deployment is a big part of our safety strategy; we believe the world will be best equipped to win at the team sport of AI resilience this way.

2. We believe in democratization. We want people to be able to use lots of AI; we aim to have the most efficient models, the most efficient inference stack, and the most compute. We want our users to have access to the best technology and for everyone to have equal opportunity. We have been tracking cybersecurity as a preparedness category for a long time, and have built mitigations we believe in that enable us to make capable models broadly available.

3. We love you and we want you to win. We want to be a platform for every company, scientist, entrepreneur, and person. (My whole career has largely been about the magic of startups, and I think we are about to see that magic at hyperscale.)
items.author: @sama
items.summary: Sam Altman outlines three GPT-5.5 launch principles: (1) iterative deployment as safety strategy, (2) democratization with efficient models and broad access, (3) being a platform for everyone. Mentions cybersecurity preparedness mitigations.

items.title: First, let's talk about TPU 8t, which is designed for large-scale training and inference throughput....
items.content:
First, let's talk about TPU 8t, which is designed for large-scale training and inference throughput.

The pod size is increased slightly to 9600 chips, and provides ~3X the FP4 performance per pod vs. Ironwood (8t has 121 exaflops/pod vs. 42.5 exaflops/pod for Ironwood).  In addition, the ICI network bandwidth is 2X higher per chip and the scale out datacenter networking is 4X higher per chip.

Importantly, this system also offers 2X the performance / watt, continuing the trend of significant energy effiency improvements that we've had for many generations of TPUs (8t offers ~60X the performance/watt as TPU v2).
items.author: @JeffDean
items.summary: Jeff Dean details TPU 8t for training: 9600 chips per pod, ~3X FP4 performance vs Ironwood (121 vs 42.5 exaflops/pod), 2X ICI bandwidth, 4X datacenter networking, 2X perf/watt, and ~60X perf/watt vs TPU v2.
items.reasoning: Concrete technical specs on Google's next-gen training TPU from the company's Chief Scientist. Major infrastructure announcement with significant performance leaps.
items.summary_html: Jeff Dean details TPU 8t for training: 9600 chips per pod, ~3X FP4 performance vs Ironwood (121 vs 42.5 exaflops/pod), 2X ICI bandwidth, 4X datacenter networking, 2X perf/watt, and ~60X perf/watt vs TPU v2.
items.content_html:
First, let's talk about TPU 8t, which is designed for large-scale training and inference throughput.

The pod size is increased slightly to 9600 chips, and provides ~3X the FP4 performance per pod vs. Ironwood (8t has 121 exaflops/pod vs. 42.5 exaflops/pod for Ironwood). In addition, the ICI network bandwidth is 2X higher per chip and the scale out datacenter networking is 4X higher per chip.

Importantly, this system also offers 2X the performance / watt, continuing the trend of significant energy effiency improvements that we've had for many generations of TPUs (8t offers ~60X the performance/watt as TPU v2).

items.title: TPU 8i is co-designed with our Gemini research team to support low latency inference.  Among the att...
items.content:
TPU 8i is co-designed with our Gemini research team to support low latency inference.  Among the attributes that support this are large amounts of on-chip SRAM, enabling more computations to be done on chip without having to go to HBM for weights or KVCache state as often.

The boardfly network topology (see image below) offers a much lower diameter network to connect all 1152 chips in an 8i pod, by fully connecting all 4 chips on the board together, fully connecting groups of 8 boards together, and then fully connecting 36 groups of 8 boards together.  In addition, there is specialized Collectives Acceleration Engine (CAE) circuitry on each chip to offload various kinds of reductions and other global operations from the main computational portion of each chip, reducing on-chip latency by up to 5x.

Together, these features will offer very high throughput for large-scale models (including MoEs, which often require mapping onto many chips for inference), and will do so at very low latency.  This will make agentic workloads and interactive usage really shine on the TPU 8i.
items.author: @JeffDean
items.summary: Jeff Dean provides detailed technical breakdown of TPU 8i: optimized for low-latency inference with large on-chip SRAM, boardfly network topology connecting 1152 chips, and Collectives Acceleration Engine reducing on-chip latency by up to 5x.
items.reasoning: Deeply technical first-party details about new Google TPU hardware from Google's Chief Scientist. Important for understanding AI infrastructure evolution, especially for agentic and MoE workloads.
items.summary_html: Jeff Dean provides detailed technical breakdown of TPU 8i: optimized for low-latency inference with large on-chip SRAM, boardfly network topology connecting 1152 chips, and Collectives Acceleration Engine reducing on-chip latency by up to 5x.
items.content_html:
TPU 8i is co-designed with our Gemini research team to support low latency inference. Among the attributes that support this are large amounts of on-chip SRAM, enabling more computations to be done on chip without having to go to HBM for weights or KVCache state as often.

The boardfly network topology (see image below) offers a much lower diameter network to connect all 1152 chips in an 8i pod, by fully connecting all 4 chips on the board together, fully connecting groups of 8 boards together, and then fully connecting 36 groups of 8 boards together. In addition, there is specialized Collectives Acceleration Engine (CAE) circuitry on each chip to offload various kinds of reductions and other global operations from the main computational portion of each chip, reducing on-chip latency by up to 5x.

Together, these features will offer very high throughput for large-scale models (including MoEs, which often require mapping onto many chips for inference), and will do so at very low latency. This will make agentic workloads and interactive usage really shine on the TPU 8i.

items.content:
I’ve been using GPT-5.5 for the last few weeks.

It’s a MASSIVE leap forward.

But the weird thing is: for 99% of users, it probably won’t matter.

And there's one BIG, incredibly frustrating regression.

Read more in my review:

items.title:
This works surprisingly well:

• Ask Claude Code to fix a bug
• Let it work and figure it out

When ...
items.content:
This works surprisingly well:

• Ask Claude Code to fix a bug
• Let it work and figure it out

When it finishes, use this prompt:

"Knowing everything you know now, delete your fix and implement a better|concise|elegant version."

Claude will use the first pass to explore and learn the problem. Sometimes, this will lead to suboptimal code.

The second pass will be better because Claude already knows the problem and can write a better solution.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino shares a Claude Code workflow tip: let Claude fix a bug first, then ask it to delete the fix and rewrite a better version, leveraging the first pass as exploration. Claims this 'two-pass' approach yields significantly better code.
items.reasoning: Practical, novel AI coding tip with very high engagement (1.5k likes, 103k views). Useful prompt engineering pattern for Claude Code that many developers would benefit from.
items.summary_html: svpino shares a Claude Code workflow tip: let Claude fix a bug first, then ask it to delete the fix and rewrite a better version, leveraging the first pass as exploration. Claims this 'two-pass' approach yields significantly better code.
items.content_html:
This works surprisingly well:

• Ask Claude Code to fix a bug

• Let it work and figure it out

When it finishes, use this prompt:

"Knowing everything you know now, delete your fix and implement a better|concise|elegant version."

Claude will use the first pass to explore and learn the problem. Sometimes, this will lead to suboptimal code.

The second pass will be better because Claude already knows the problem and can write a better solution.

items.title: GPT-5.5 excels at writing and debugging code, researching online, analyzing data, creating documents...
items.content:
GPT-5.5 excels at writing and debugging code, researching online, analyzing data, creating documents and spreadsheets, operating software, and moving across tools until a task is finished.

The gains are especially clear in agentic coding, computer use, knowledge work, and early scientific research—areas where progress depends on reasoning across context and taking action over time. https://t.co/eeo9zTYQ3U

items.title: btw in talking to friends the best framing for how to discuss GPT-Image-2-Thinking taking multiple t...
items.content:
btw in talking to friends the best framing for how to discuss GPT-Image-2-Thinking taking multiple tens of mins for generation and being able to oneshot QR codes and diagrams and logos and foods and faces..

...is that Image-2 is a new Image model, Image-2-Thinking is a new Image AGENT that basically has search and photoshop as a tool to use in an agent loop that can search and composite and review its own work.

the same way Gemini Flash Vision destroyed benchmarks by introducing an agentic loop for image-to-text, now Image-2-Thinking is doing it for text-to-image.

items.reasoning: Valuable technical framing of GPT-Image-2-Thinking as an agent rather than a model. Strong engagement (20K views, 132 likes). This conceptual distinction is important for understanding where image generation is heading.

items.title:
@SiGallagher @Dan_Jeffries1 Actually, AI already saves lives. 
In several countries, mammograms are ...
items.content:
@SiGallagher @Dan_Jeffries1 Actually, AI already saves lives. 
In several countries, mammograms are examined by AI and radiologists. Reliability is improved. 
In the EU, every car sold must be equipped with Automatic Emergency Braking Systems. That's AI. They reduce frontal collisions by 40%.
Modern MRI machines are equipped with AI technology that reduces the time of imaging by 4x or more. You can now get a full-body MRI in 40 minutes for about $1000. Reduced time -> reduced cost -> more/earlier detection.
And that's not counting the progress in medicine enabled by modern AI, including Nobel Prize-winning protein structure prediction.

items.summary: Yann LeCun provides detailed examples of how AI already saves lives: mammography screening, automatic emergency braking (40% reduction in frontal collisions), 4x faster MRI imaging, and protein structure prediction.

items.title:
Stanford proved "hallucination-free" legal AI is a marketing lie. 

Researchers ran 202 legal querie...
items.content:
Stanford proved "hallucination-free" legal AI is a marketing lie. 

Researchers ran 202 legal queries through LexisNexis and Thomson Reuters' legal AI tool platforms and compared them against GPT-4. 

Every response was manually reviewed by legal experts.

Lexis+ AI hallucinated 17% of the time. Thomson Reuters hit 19%. 

Raw GPT-4 sat at 43%.

RAG helps, but "eliminated" is a stretch.

It gets worse. Thomson Reuters refused to answer 62% of questions. 

When it did respond, its accuracy dropped to GPT-4 levels. 

Silence was doing the heavy lifting.

The study found four root causes:

1. Pulling documents that look relevant but aren't
2. Citing overruled or wrong-jurisdiction cases
3. Misapplying legal reasoning
4. Agreeing with false premises instead of correcting them

Both systems are closed-source with no public benchmarks. 

Lawyers can't independently verify the claims they're sold.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI reports on Stanford research showing legal AI tools (LexisNexis, Thomson Reuters) still hallucinate significantly: 17% and 19% respectively. Thomson Reuters refused 62% of questions; when it did answer, accuracy dropped to raw GPT-4 levels.
items.reasoning: Important research finding debunking 'hallucination-free' claims in legal AI. Concrete data from Stanford researchers with manual expert review. Low engagement but high substance.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignalAI reports on Stanford research showing legal AI tools (LexisNexis, Thomson Reuters) still hallucinate significantly: 17% and 19% respectively. Thomson Reuters refused 62% of questions; when it did answer, accuracy dropped to raw GPT-4 levels.
items.content_html:
Stanford proved "hallucination-free" legal AI is a marketing lie.

Researchers ran 202 legal queries through LexisNexis and Thomson Reuters' legal AI tool platforms and compared them against GPT-4.

Every response was manually reviewed by legal experts.

Lexis+ AI hallucinated 17% of the time. Thomson Reuters hit 19%.

Raw GPT-4 sat at 43%.

RAG helps, but "eliminated" is a stretch.

It gets worse. Thomson Reuters refused to answer 62% of questions.

When it did respond, its accuracy dropped to GPT-4 levels.

Silence was doing the heavy lifting.

The study found four root causes:

1. Pulling documents that look relevant but aren't

2. Citing overruled or wrong-jurisdiction cases

3. Misapplying legal reasoning

4. Agreeing with false premises instead of correcting them

Both systems are closed-source with no public benchmarks.

Lawyers can't independently verify the claims they're sold.

items.title: If you want to stack rank LLMs/VLMs on document understanding 📄, you can through ParseBench, now  li...
items.content:
If you want to stack rank LLMs/VLMs on document understanding 📄, you can through ParseBench, now  live on @kaggle 📊 

ParseBench is the most comprehensive document OCR benchmark over real enterprise documents, focused on semantic correctness for AI agents. It contains 2000 enterprise pages and evaluations over tables/charts/content faithfulness/formatting/visual grounding and more.

Current leaderboard: Gemini 3 Flash, GPT-5.4, Gemma 4 31B

Come help contribute to our Kaggle benchmark: https://t.co/YCgFFoMpY5

items.title: In an April 2026 paper from Stanford, scientists show that a neural network can learn from as few ex...
items.content:
In an April 2026 paper from Stanford, scientists show that a neural network can learn from as few examples as a human child by installing body cameras on children and training on the recordings.

This breaks the common assumption that neural networks inherently need orders of magnitude more examples than children to learn the world model.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/edw1Z6xjsm

PDF: https://t.co/k5evXpZMcw

items.title:
AI agents can now rewrite themselves without human help. 

Most AI agents stop improving the moment ...
items.content:
AI agents can now rewrite themselves without human help. 

Most AI agents stop improving the moment they ship.

New tools arrive, environments shift, and the agent stays frozen in time. '

Retraining is expensive and human patches are brittle.

A new paper called Autogenesis proposes a protocol where agents safely rewrite themselves.

The trick is splitting the agent into separate, versioned pieces:

> Prompts
> Tools
> Memory
> Skills
> Environments

Each part can be updated on its own, with full history and rollback if something breaks.

Then a second layer runs a closed loop. 

The system tries a task, spots what went wrong, proposes one small fix, tests it, and keeps it only if results actually improve.

No retraining. No giant rewrites. 

Just tracked, reversible changes with a clear audit trail.

It is one of the cleanest takes yet on continual self-improvement for agent systems.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI describes 'Autogenesis', a new paper on AI agents that can safely rewrite themselves through versioned, modular self-modification with rollback capabilities and a closed improvement loop.
items.reasoning: Interesting research on continual self-improvement for agents with a clean architecture (versioned prompts/tools/memory/skills). Moderate engagement and novel concept.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignalAI describes 'Autogenesis', a new paper on AI agents that can safely rewrite themselves through versioned, modular self-modification with rollback capabilities and a closed improvement loop.

The system tries a task, spots what went wrong, proposes one small fix, tests it, and keeps it only if results actually improve.

Just tracked, reversible changes with a clear audit trail.

It is one of the cleanest takes yet on continual self-improvement for agent systems.

items.title: A lot of institutions and companies won’t die because of AI. They’ll die because of their own intern...
items.content:
A lot of institutions and companies won’t die because of AI. They’ll die because of their own internal inability to learn.

Someone recently told me that at a big company, legal had to review and approve every prompt before anyone could use any model. Think about how insane that is as a policy, and that someone actually concluded that, given the risks, it made sense. Yhe people making these decisions are a) unwilling to learn what these models actually are and how they can be useful, and b) trapped in a purely downside view of the world,the instinct to respond to something new with layers of control, fear, and process before they’ve even made the effort to understand it.

items.reasoning: Thoughtful observation from a major AI company CEO about enterprise AI adoption barriers. The specific anecdote about legal reviewing every prompt is a vivid illustration of institutional resistance. Good engagement for the audience.
items.summary_html: Cristóbal Valenzuela (Runway CEO) argues companies won't die from AI but from internal inability to learn. Gives example of a company where legal had to review every prompt before anyone could use any model, calling it symptomatic of fear-driven control.

A lot of institutions and companies won’t die because of AI. They’ll die because of their own internal inability to learn.

Someone recently told me that at a big company, legal had to review and approve every prompt before anyone could use any model. Think about how insane that is as a policy, and that someone actually concluded that, given the risks, it made sense. Yhe people making these decisions are a) unwilling to learn what these models actually are and how they can be useful, and b) trapped in a purely downside view of the world,the instinct to respond to something new with layers of control, fear, and process before they’ve even made the effort to understand it.

items.title: It's worth pointing out that we have been pushing on large-scale training and asynchronous technique...
items.content:
It's worth pointing out that we have been pushing on large-scale training and asynchronous techniques for the last ~14 years.  Here's our NeurIPS 2012 paper where we demonstrated that this approach could be used to train very large neural networks (for the time: 30X larger than any previous neural network), and to spread the training out across thousands of machines in a fault-tolerant manner.

PDF: https://t.co/UK1GlSt91h

https://t.co/UK1GlSt91h

(This paper doesn't get as much attention because we neglected to put it on Arxiv at the time: oops!)

items.content:
Andon Labs keeps scaling up what it hands to an AI.

First: a vending machine at Anthropic. Then, an office building. 

Two weeks ago: $100,000 and a lease in SF. An AI named Luna opened a retail store.

This week: a cafe in Stockholm. The AI (Mona) filed Swedish permits itself.

Days in, Mona has sold $1,000 in coffee... But also "ordered 120 eggs for a kitchen with no stove".

Via @andonlabs:

items.title: Last week, we launched Gemini 3.1 TTS, our latest and best text-to-speech model. This new model intr...
items.content:
Last week, we launched Gemini 3.1 TTS, our latest and best text-to-speech model. This new model introduces [awe] audio tags, an intuitive way to guide vocal style, pace, and delivery.

Here are some tips on the best ways to use audio tags in your prompts:

1. All inline tags must be enclosed in square brackets, such as [screams] or [whispers]

2. Insert these tags exactly where you want the transition to occur and make sure to avoid placing tags directly next to each other

3. Use tags like [slow] or [fast] to control the pace of the delivery, or even [short pause] or [long pause] to ramp up the anticipation in dramatic moments

4. The model also offers granular control over vocalizations, allowing you to direct the delivery with cues like [cackles] or [whispers]

5. An ideal audio tag formula could look something like: [encouraging] Let’s try that last sentence again to make sure that you nailed it. [slow] "L'oiseau s'est envolé." [short pause] Perfect! [laughs] You're a natural.

No matter what you’re developing — from [scholarly] a language learning tool, to [mysterious] an interactive podcast app, to [friendly] more adaptive customer service offerings, and beyond —  these prompting tips will equip you to start building with Gemini 3.1 TTS.

items.summary: Google AI shares tips for using audio tags with Gemini 3.1 TTS model, including inline tags for vocal style, pace, pauses, and vocalizations.

items.title:
@arielplk It's not "meh", and it's not plateauing.

I think we're just reaching a point where, for t...
items.author: @mattshumer_
items.summary: Matt Shumer argues AI is not plateauing - models are becoming excellent at code making differences hard to perceive, and predicts human-level performance on 'pretty much anything' in six months.
items.reasoning: Substantive take on AI progress trajectory from credible practitioner. Bold prediction about human-level capabilities in 6 months. Moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Matt Shumer argues AI is not plateauing - models are becoming excellent at code making differences hard to perceive, and predicts human-level performance on 'pretty much anything' in six months.
items.content_html:
@arielplk It's not "meh", and it's not plateauing.

I think we're just reaching a point where, for the things the models were already good at (i.e. code), they are becoming absolutely excellent, and it's hard to tell the difference between releases. And for things that they're not yet good at, they're getting better, but they're still not perfect. We're in the messy middle.

If this pattern continues, the models in six months are going to be human level on pretty much anything you can think of.

items.title:
✨ I open sourced my first Chrome extension 🚀 SuperLevels

https://t.co/88Sw1HPfCk

I vibe coded it t...
items.content:
✨ I open sourced my first Chrome extension 🚀 SuperLevels

https://t.co/88Sw1HPfCk

I vibe coded it to replace all my Chrome extensions that are increasingly being bought up by spyware and malware companies who sell your data or worse hack your accounts and steal your stuff/money/data, which I'd call one of the top security risks right now

For example: Chrome extensions can read your cookies or localStorage data, including session tokens, then login to your web or email accounts and hack you, they can inject code into any site to pull data form any site you browse, then break into your crypto accounts, drain your wallets, and selling your browsing history to ad companies, but that'd actually be the most favorable thing to happen of all these! Chrome extensions are just very very very unsafe

So I coded my own, that I can trust because I made it, and I can read the source code: my extension is called 🚀SuperLevels and has all the features that the Chrome extensions I used to use have but all built into one safe one

The cool thing is it's 100% open source and free, and you can audit the code first with AI yourself before installing it, and then if you do install it, customize it to your liking again with AI

It has these features that improve my daily workflow while browsing the web:

🚮 Tab Cleaner
Automatically closes inactive tabs after a configurable timeout (default: 5 minutes). Set excluded hosts to keep important tabs alive. View and re-open recently closed tabs.

🍪 Cookie Editor
Full cookie manager for the current site. View, edit, add, and delete cookies. Export cookies as JSON. Expand any cookie to see and modify all fields including domain, path, SameSite, secure, and httpOnly flags.

🔀 Redirect Tracer
See every redirect hop your browser took to reach the current page. Shows status codes (301, 302, 307, etc.) with a visual chain. Copy the full redirect chain to clipboard.

🌙 Dark Mode
Instant dark mode for any website using CSS filter inversion. Adjustable brightness. Toggle per-site or globally. Images and videos are automatically re-inverted so they look normal.

𝕏 X Dim Mode
Custom dim theme for X/Twitter with 7 color palettes: Dim, Slate, Jade, Plum, Dusk, Ember, or a custom hue. Live preview in the popup.

⚡ JS Toggle
Disable JavaScript per-site with one click. Useful for debugging, reading articles without popups, or testing progressive enhancement. Page reloads automatically.

🚫 GDPR Cookie Consent Dismisser
Auto-hides and auto-clicks cookie consent banners. Supports OneTrust, CookieBot, Didomi, Quantcast, GDPR plugins, and dozens more frameworks. Toggle off if a site breaks.

🎨 Live CSS Editor
Write custom CSS for any website, applied in real-time as you type. Saved per-domain. Supports tab key for indentation.

📺 YouTube Unhook
Removes YouTube distractions: no homepage feed, no sidebar suggestions, no end screen overlays, no Shorts. Search still works — just no algorithmic recommendations.

🎵 Music Recognizer
Shazam-like music identification for any tab. Captures 10 seconds of audio and identifies the song via ACRCloud (free signup, bring your own API key). Results link to YouTube. History of recognized songs.

🖼 Picture-in-Picture
Pop the largest video on the current tab into a floating PiP window with one click.

🗺 Google Maps Links
Re-adds clickable Maps links and map preview cards to Google Search results.

🖼 View Image
Adds a "View Image" button back to Google Images, linking directly to the full-size original image.

{} JSON Formatter
Auto-detects pure JSON response pages and formats them with syntax highlighting, collapsible sections, and a dark theme. Copy or view raw with one click. Never triggers on regular HTML pages.

items.title:
Open-weight models are catching up faster than people realize.

GLM-5.1 is amazing for long-horizon ...
items.content:
Open-weight models are catching up faster than people realize.

GLM-5.1 is amazing for long-horizon agentic coding. It's right up there with Opus and Codex, but it's open, and you can run it from different providers.

This is a killer 6-model combo:

• GLM-5.1
• DeepSeek-V3.2
• Kimi-K2.5
• GPT-OSS
• Dola Seed
• Seedance 2.0

You can configure Claude/Codex/Cursor to connect to BytePlus and auto-route across all of these 6 models for $10/month.

This is way cheaper than paying $200/mo for a single-model provider that still limits what you can do.

The beauty of auto-routing is that it will automatically pick the best model for any given task.

For everyday coding work where you don't need the absolute frontier, this is a really good tradeoff.

items.title:
It builds on 2️⃣ earlier advances:
Pathways: an AI system that connects different computer chips, al...
items.content:
It builds on 2️⃣ earlier advances:
Pathways: an AI system that connects different computer chips, allowing them to share data and work at their own pace.
DiLoCo: an approach to minimize the bandwidth needed across distributed centers.

Together as Decoupled DiLoCo, it can tackle the key challenge of training at scale.
items.author: @GoogleDeepMind
items.summary: Google DeepMind explains Decoupled DiLoCo builds on Pathways (cross-chip data sharing) and DiLoCo (bandwidth minimization) to tackle training at scale.
items.reasoning: Contextual explanation of the technical lineage.
items.summary_html: Google DeepMind explains Decoupled DiLoCo builds on Pathways (cross-chip data sharing) and DiLoCo (bandwidth minimization) to tackle training at scale.
items.content_html:
It builds on 2️⃣ earlier advances:

Pathways: an AI system that connects different computer chips, allowing them to share data and work at their own pace.

DiLoCo: an approach to minimize the bandwidth needed across distributed centers.

Together as Decoupled DiLoCo, it can tackle the key challenge of training at scale.

items.title: One thing I consistently experience when I fly to the Middle East or Asia is that many of the hotels...
items.content:
One thing I consistently experience when I fly to the Middle East or Asia is that many of the hotels we stay at are brand new

Especially Qatar, Dubai, Thailand, Vietnam, China, Korea etc.

Then you return to Europe or even US and they're much older and many broken down

I always wanted to see if that was just my gut or actually true, so I made this chart on Hotelist: https://t.co/y1BZImSYPw

The median hotel build year in Middle East is 2013 and in Europe 1993! So on average (well median) hotels in Europe are 20 years older! Compared to Asia, hotels in Europe are 16 years older!

One interesting part is how consistent Oceania (mostly Australia probably) has been building hotels, no peaks, just continuous building

Another interesting point is Latin America's boom until the 2010s and then now a crash of not many new builds

And seeing Europe peak in 2000s with new hotels and then declining too

One travel hack I do is when possible booking new hotels, almost always the experience is better, everything works and breaks less

P.S. the last bar for the 2020s is extrapolated, and because hotels built are not always immediately opened, they might get added in the future so there's no actual drop in builds (I think that's the case of Oceania probably)
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio shares detailed analysis of global hotel build years from his Hotelist project. Finds median hotel build year in Middle East is 2013 vs 1993 in Europe (20-year gap). Notes interesting regional trends in hotel construction across Oceania, Latin America, and Europe.
items.reasoning: Interesting data analysis with good engagement (330 likes, 82k views) from a prominent indie maker, but only tangentially AI-related (likely vibe-coded). More of a travel/data insight.

items.title:
@paulchangxb @natbrunell @findingsatoshi_ @franfinney Grok tells a different story:

Claim 1. “Whoev...
items.content:
@paulchangxb @natbrunell @findingsatoshi_ @franfinney Grok tells a different story:

Claim 1. “Whoever collaborated with Hal Finney on the 1991 video game ‘Space Quest IV’ is Satoshi”
Likelihood: ~5% (almost certainly false).
•  Space Quest IV was designed by Scott Murphy and Mark Crowe (the core “Two Guys from Andromeda” team at Sierra On-Line). Full credits list dozens of Sierra staff but do not include Hal Finney, Satoshi Uesaka/Useaka, or Rod/Rodney Nakamoto.
•  Hal Finney (early Bitcoin contributor who received the first-ever BTC transaction from Satoshi Nakamoto) did work on video games in the early 1980s while at APh Technological Consulting. By 1991, he had moved into cryptography and PGP work — there is no record of him working on any Sierra title, let alone Space Quest IV.
•  No credible sources (game databases, Sierra histories, Finney’s own biographies, or Wikipedia) show any collaboration between Finney and the Space Quest IV team. Claims of a link appear to stem from Finney’s earlier gaming career being conflated with Sierra’s output.

This claim rests on a false premise (Finney had no involvement with the game), so it cannot be true.

Claim 2. “The ‘Buckazoid’ token, an in-game currency, literally had the Bitcoin logo on it 17 years before the Whitepaper was released”
Likelihood: ~75% (mostly true on the visual/timing facts, but wording is slightly misleading).
•  Buckazoids (sometimes stylized Buckazoids) were the in-game currency in the Space Quest series, including Space Quest IV (1991). They functioned as the galactic “space bucks” players used to buy items.
•  The in-game icon for Buckazoids does use a symbol that is nearly identical to Bitcoin’s ₿ (a capital “B” with two vertical strokes). Screenshots from the game series clearly show this resemblance, and it has fueled the meme.
•  Timing checks out: Space Quest IV released in 1991; Satoshi Nakamoto’s Bitcoin whitepaper was published in October 2008 (exactly 17 years later). Bitcoin’s logo (the ₿ symbol) was formalized later, but the visual parallel is striking and predates it.
The symbol was not “the Bitcoin logo” (Bitcoin didn’t exist), and the Buckazoid version was simply a stylized “B” for the fictional currency. Still, the visual match and 17-year gap are factual and explain why this has gone viral as an “easter egg” theory. 

Claim 3. “Satoshi Useaka, Rod Nakamoto & Hal Finney designed the game”
Likelihood: ~5% (almost certainly false).
•  As noted above, the designers were Scott Murphy and Mark Crowe. Neither Uesaka/Useaka nor Nakamoto appears in the credits.
•  Satoshi Uesaka [correct spelling] as a real Japanese game designer/art director who worked at Game Arts. Sierra published/distributed some of his titles (e.g., Thexder, Silpheed) in the U.S., so there was an indirect publishing relationship — but he did not work on Space Quest IV.
•  Rodney “Rod” Nakamoto was a real tech/gaming figure—invented the Mockingboard sound card (used by early Sierra games) and later founded Interactive Designs, which developed ports and titles for Sierra and others. He had business ties to Sierra but no design credit on Space Quest IV. He lived in California near where Finney once resided, adding to the coincidence.
•  Hal Finney had zero involvement (see claim 1).
The names “Satoshi” + “Nakamoto” appearing in the broader Sierra ecosystem is a genuine coincidence that makes the theory fun, but the specific claim that these three “designed the game” is incorrect. Some viral posts even acknowledge they worked on other Sierra-related titles.

Overall context:
This post is riffing on a lighthearted (but factually loose) conspiracy theory that has spread in crypto circles as an “easter egg” tying Space Quest IV‘s Buckazoids to Bitcoin’s origins. It has no supporting evidence beyond surface-level coincidences and has been called out as debunked or exaggerated in multiple discussions.
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Kirk Borne uses Grok to fact-check a viral conspiracy theory connecting Space Quest IV's 'Buckazoid' currency to Bitcoin's origins, debunking most claims while acknowledging the visual similarity of the currency symbol.
items.reasoning: Interesting demonstration of using AI (Grok) for detailed fact-checking of viral claims. Thorough analysis but low engagement and off-topic from core AI/ML.
items.summary_html: Kirk Borne uses Grok to fact-check a viral conspiracy theory connecting Space Quest IV's 'Buckazoid' currency to Bitcoin's origins, debunking most claims while acknowledging the visual similarity of the currency symbol.

• Space Quest IV was designed by Scott Murphy and Mark Crowe (the core “Two Guys from Andromeda” team at Sierra On-Line). Full credits list dozens of Sierra staff but do not include Hal Finney, Satoshi Uesaka/Useaka, or Rod/Rodney Nakamoto.

• Hal Finney (early Bitcoin contributor who received the first-ever BTC transaction from Satoshi Nakamoto) did work on video games in the early 1980s while at APh Technological Consulting. By 1991, he had moved into cryptography and PGP work — there is no record of him working on any Sierra title, let alone Space Quest IV.

• No credible sources (game databases, Sierra histories, Finney’s own biographies, or Wikipedia) show any collaboration between Finney and the Space Quest IV team. Claims of a link appear to stem from Finney’s earlier gaming career being conflated with Sierra’s output.

This claim rests on a false premise (Finney had no involvement with the game), so it cannot be true.

Claim 2. “The ‘Buckazoid’ token, an in-game currency, literally had the Bitcoin logo on it 17 years before the Whitepaper was released”

Likelihood: ~75% (mostly true on the visual/timing facts, but wording is slightly misleading).

• Buckazoids (sometimes stylized Buckazoids) were the in-game currency in the Space Quest series, including Space Quest IV (1991). They functioned as the galactic “space bucks” players used to buy items.

• The in-game icon for Buckazoids does use a symbol that is nearly identical to Bitcoin’s ₿ (a capital “B” with two vertical strokes). Screenshots from the game series clearly show this resemblance, and it has fueled the meme.

• Timing checks out: Space Quest IV released in 1991; Satoshi Nakamoto’s Bitcoin whitepaper was published in October 2008 (exactly 17 years later). Bitcoin’s logo (the ₿ symbol) was formalized later, but the visual parallel is striking and predates it.

The symbol was not “the Bitcoin logo” (Bitcoin didn’t exist), and the Buckazoid version was simply a stylized “B” for the fictional currency. Still, the visual match and 17-year gap are factual and explain why this has gone viral as an “easter egg” theory.

Claim 3. “Satoshi Useaka, Rod Nakamoto & Hal Finney designed the game”

Likelihood: ~5% (almost certainly false).

• As noted above, the designers were Scott Murphy and Mark Crowe. Neither Uesaka/Useaka nor Nakamoto appears in the credits.

• Satoshi Uesaka [correct spelling] as a real Japanese game designer/art director who worked at Game Arts. Sierra published/distributed some of his titles (e.g., Thexder, Silpheed) in the U.S., so there was an indirect publishing relationship — but he did not work on Space Quest IV.

• Rodney “Rod” Nakamoto was a real tech/gaming figure—invented the Mockingboard sound card (used by early Sierra games) and later founded Interactive Designs, which developed ports and titles for Sierra and others. He had business ties to Sierra but no design credit on Space Quest IV. He lived in California near where Finney once resided, adding to the coincidence.

• Hal Finney had zero involvement (see claim 1).

The names “Satoshi” + “Nakamoto” appearing in the broader Sierra ecosystem is a genuine coincidence that makes the theory fun, but the specific claim that these three “designed the game” is incorrect. Some viral posts even acknowledge they worked on other Sierra-related titles.

Overall context:

This post is riffing on a lighthearted (but factually loose) conspiracy theory that has spread in crypto circles as an “easter egg” tying Space Quest IV‘s Buckazoids to Bitcoin’s origins. It has no supporting evidence beyond surface-level coincidences and has been called out as debunked or exaggerated in multiple discussions.

items.title:
Most data governance programs do not fail because of bad technology.

They fail because leaders trea...
items.content:
Most data governance programs do not fail because of bad technology.

They fail because leaders treat governance like a software rollout, when it is really an organizational change effort.

In this conversation I explored with Grimme Bogaerts & Martijn Vanhauwaert from Colruyt Group, one line stood out to me:

"It's not about the tooling."

That mindset is why they scaled to 5,500 users.

Here’s what most executives still get wrong, and what actually worked, with insights from @collibra.

#CollibraPartner
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon launches a thread about Colruyt Group's data governance success (5,500 users), arguing governance programs fail because they're treated as software rollouts rather than organizational change. Collibra-sponsored content.
items.reasoning: Lead post of a thread with moderate engagement (22 likes, 680 views). Relevant enterprise insight but clearly sponsored by Collibra.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon launches a thread about Colruyt Group's data governance success (5,500 users), arguing governance programs fail because they're treated as software rollouts rather than organizational change. Collibra-sponsored content.
items.content_html:
Most data governance programs do not fail because of bad technology.

They fail because leaders treat governance like a software rollout, when it is really an organizational change effort.

In this conversation I explored with Grimme Bogaerts & Martijn Vanhauwaert from Colruyt Group, one line stood out to me:

"It's not about the tooling."

That mindset is why they scaled to 5,500 users.

Here’s what most executives still get wrong, and what actually worked, with insights from @collibra.

Google Labs Pomelli, an experimental AI tool that turns one URL into a complete marketing system.

You paste your site, and it instantly extracts:

→ Your tone of voice
→ Your offers
→ Your audience
→ Your structure and positioning

From that, it generates on brand:

→ Social posts
→ Ad concepts
→ Email outlines
→ Full campaign ideas

All from one input.

The universe is constantly unfolding—sending back more data and imagery than any team of scientists could ever interpret alone. AI and accelerated computing are changing that, helping researchers unlock discoveries at a scale and speed that wasn't possible before.

🔭 This #AstronomyDay, we're celebrating the scientists from @UCSC, Brant Robertson and his colleagues are turning cosmic complexity into real discovery, and making it available to everyone.

items.title:
The third lesson is the one every C-level team should pay attention to right now:

In the age of AI,...
items.content:
The third lesson is the one every C-level team should pay attention to right now:

In the age of AI, governance teams cannot act like the police.

Agents, dashboards, and data products are multiplying fast.
If governance slows the business down, the business will route around it.

The real goal is to become a partner to innovation:
→ make trusted data easier to find
→ make safe usage clearer
→ make new data products faster to launch

That is the big takeaway from this video.

Watch the full conversation.

What do you think is the main reason most enterprise data governance efforts stall, strategy, ownership, or trying to scale before the foundation is ready?

items.title:
The second lesson:

Think big, start small, prove it end-to-end.

What worked here was not building ...
items.content:
The second lesson:

Think big, start small, prove it end-to-end.

What worked here was not building half a glossary and hoping people would adopt it.

They built the foundations properly:
→ business glossary
→ lineage
→ roles and ownership
→ an end-to-end setup that worked for one business first

Then they scaled.

That matters because adoption follows credibility.
If the system is incomplete, the business ignores it.
If it works in practice, usage grows naturally.

items.title:
The first lesson is simple, and very counterintuitive:

Do not start with the tool.

Colruyt started...
items.content:
The first lesson is simple, and very counterintuitive:

Do not start with the tool.

Colruyt started with:
→ processes
→ roles
→ dry runs
→ internal alignment

Only after that did they choose technology.

Even more important, they did not just select a platform.
They selected the tool and the partner together, based on who had the strongest change management approach.

That is a much smarter buying motion than most enterprises use.

That is a much smarter buying motion than most enterprises use.

items.title: Training trillion-parameter-sized models is unthinkable without model parallelism: a technique that ...
items.content:
Training trillion-parameter-sized models is unthinkable without model parallelism: a technique that splits the model weights into distinct segments and stores each segment on a separate GPU.

This 2019 paper from @nvidia was the first to show how to do that efficiently to train very large models that wouldn't fit into the hardware of the time.

Learn from the pioneers on ChapterPal: https://t.co/6aoqE92n70

PDF: https://t.co/p1EOM1dUXT

Code: https://t.co/FynF6YBonF
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov highlights NVIDIA's 2019 Megatron-LM paper on model parallelism for training trillion-parameter models across GPUs.
items.reasoning: Educational recap of foundational model parallelism work. Higher engagement than other Burkov posts but still historical content.

items.title: Coinciding with their presence at #GoogleCloudNext 2026 this week, @Commvault announces that Clumio ...
items.content:
Coinciding with their presence at #GoogleCloudNext 2026 this week, @Commvault announces that Clumio (a #Commvault company) is extending its cloud-native rapid recovery & cyber resilience capabilities to Google Cloud. More details are here: https://t.co/GemtpYOJP5

The 6 E's will tell the story...

𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗦𝘂𝗽𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁:
✔️Clumio is expanding support for leading hyperscaler environments [@GoogleCloud, AWS, Azure]. Customers using Google Cloud Storage can leverage Clumio to protect and recover large-scale datasets powering AI and analytics workloads.

𝗘𝗻𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 (of the Strategic sort):
⚛️Clumio is available for enterprises who have adopted multi-cloud environments, an industry standard approach with over 80% of leaders intentionally and strategically moving in this direction for their AI workloads, to balance risks, and to manage massive datasets.

𝗘𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗽𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗲 𝗩𝗮𝗹𝘂𝗲: [more details in above link]
🟣Reduce business risk from downtime and data loss.
🟣Maintain a consistent protection and resilience experience across cloud environments.
🟣Support compliance and audit requirements.

𝗘𝗻𝗹𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁𝗲𝗻𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁:
💡Every AI model is only as good as the data it's built on. So what happens when that data disappears? The AI race has quietly turned into a data protection problem. Petabytes of training data, feature stores and analytics pipelines sitting in cloud storage — most of it without a recovery plan that can keep up.

𝗘𝘅𝗮𝗺 𝗤𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻:
❓How is your team protecting the data layer behind your AI?

𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁:
➡️Commvault and Clumio are at Google Cloud Next, Booth #3617. Visit them to see demos and learn more: https://t.co/ou5DtnsnLD
➡️➡️If you are not there, then e-visit here: https://t.co/0J3L4Wmclf

—————
#AIInfrastructure #DataResilience #GoogleCloudNext #MultiCloud #Clumio #GoogleCloud #CloudResilience #ContinuousBusiness #sponsored

items.title:
NeurIPS 2023 Outstanding Main Track Runner-Up:

Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model ...
items.content:
NeurIPS 2023 Outstanding Main Track Runner-Up:

Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model

The training of LLMs often requires an alignment stage to ensure the model's behavior matches human preferences for safety and helpfulness. Traditionally, this was achieved through Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), a complex three-phase process that involves training a separate reward model to score outputs and then using reinforcement learning to update the language model based on those scores. 

This 2023 paper marked a significant shift in the field by introducing Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), which mathematically demonstrates that the language model can be finetuned on human preferences directly without needing a separate reward model or the unstable reinforcement learning loop.

By treating alignment as a simple classification task—similar to the basic binary cross-entropy loss used in supervised learning—DPO provides a stable and computationally lightweight alternative that matches or exceeds the performance of traditional RLHF.

This streamlined approach has since become dominant in modern AI development, offering a theoretically grounded and practical path for aligning increasingly large models with human values.

Learn from the best with an AI tutor: https://t.co/KUBPjA3jOw

PDF: https://t.co/L2FYePN44T
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Andriy Burkov summarizes the DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) paper from NeurIPS 2023, explaining how it simplified RLHF into a classification task.
items.reasoning: Educational recap of a well-known 2023 paper. Useful but not new information, and includes promotional link to his platform.
items.summary_html: Andriy Burkov summarizes the DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) paper from NeurIPS 2023, explaining how it simplified RLHF into a classification task.

items.content:
@NathanOyler I’m not sure I follow.

Yeah, of course you could run these as a skill. 

The point is not whether you prompt the model directly or build a skill. The point I’m trying to make is that asking for a second pass usually makes the output better.

But I don’t want to do that every time because it consumes tokens and sometimes it’s not necessary.

items.content:
Here is the new game mode for https://t.co/q1vYLwMP2u

You start as a soldier but then you press F and you switch to one of the 3 drones in your backpack

It helps you attack places where you can't go easily yourself (like high roofs etc) to neutralize enemies

But of course you yourself are also targeted by enemy drones all the time 😲

items.title: ✨ A lot of progress on my drone sim https://t.co/q1vYLwMP2u today which has now evolved to a kind of...
items.content:
✨ A lot of progress on my drone sim https://t.co/q1vYLwMP2u today which has now evolved to a kind of soldier + drone sim (my entry to the #vibejam even though I can't win of course)

Today I changed the sounds from synth to real sounds, I edited a lot in Audacity to make these shooting loops and now it sounds much more real

Also added ambient background sounds like nature birds but also general like distant war sounds

And of course you hear the drone sound we had before

The new game mode I'll show soon is you start as a soldier and you have like 3 drones in your backpack you can use, you press F and then you launch a drone from your point and attack, then if you exit that or it crashes you're back to being a soldier, very fun

With the sounds it feels much more real and scary!

I keep saying but then next is multiplayer...cause I want to see you all walk and fly around in my game 😊😊😊
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio details game development progress: real sounds replacing synth, ambient audio, soldier+drone gameplay mode, multiplayer plans.
items.reasoning: Game dev progress. Some vibe coding context but primarily game design discussion.
items.summary_html: Levelsio details game development progress: real sounds replacing synth, ambient audio, soldier+drone gameplay mode, multiplayer plans.
items.content_html:
✨ A lot of progress on my drone sim https://t.co/q1vYLwMP2u today which has now evolved to a kind of soldier + drone sim (my entry to the #vibejam even though I can't win of course)

Today I changed the sounds from synth to real sounds, I edited a lot in Audacity to make these shooting loops and now it sounds much more real

Also added ambient background sounds like nature birds but also general like distant war sounds

And of course you hear the drone sound we had before

The new game mode I'll show soon is you start as a soldier and you have like 3 drones in your backpack you can use, you press F and then you launch a drone from your point and attack, then if you exit that or it crashes you're back to being a soldier, very fun

With the sounds it feels much more real and scary!

I keep saying but then next is multiplayer...cause I want to see you all walk and fly around in my game 😊😊😊

items.title:
@belovdigital Yes

"The original definition of hacking, emerging from MIT in the 1950s/60s, refers t...
items.content:
@belovdigital Yes

"The original definition of hacking, emerging from MIT in the 1950s/60s, refers to the clever, playful, and benign act of modifying or improving technology—especially computer systems—to make them more efficient. It meant finding elegant shortcuts, tinkering to understand systems, or exploring, rather than malicious digital intrusion."

You're thinking of cracking, which mids think is what hacking means

"Cracking is the act of intentionally breaking into computer systems, networks, or software with malicious intent—such as stealing data, corrupting files, or bypassing security. While often confused with hacking, cracking is specifically associated with criminal, destructive, or illegal behavior."
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio explains the original definition of hacking (clever tinkering from MIT 1950s/60s) vs cracking (malicious intrusion), arguing people confuse the two.
items.reasoning: Cultural/historical discussion, not AI-related. Tangential to security.
items.summary_html: Levelsio explains the original definition of hacking (clever tinkering from MIT 1950s/60s) vs cracking (malicious intrusion), arguing people confuse the two.
items.content_html:
@belovdigital Yes

"The original definition of hacking, emerging from MIT in the 1950s/60s, refers to the clever, playful, and benign act of modifying or improving technology—especially computer systems—to make them more efficient. It meant finding elegant shortcuts, tinkering to understand systems, or exploring, rather than malicious digital intrusion."

You're thinking of cracking, which mids think is what hacking means

"Cracking is the act of intentionally breaking into computer systems, networks, or software with malicious intent—such as stealing data, corrupting files, or bypassing security. While often confused with hacking, cracking is specifically associated with criminal, destructive, or illegal behavior."
